sections.title: Key Greek Particles
sections.explanation: Greek particles are small, uninflected words that carry grammatical meaning. The most important are the mood markers: θα (future/conditional marker, placed before a verb), να (subjunctive marker, used for wishes, purpose, and after many verbs), and ας (let's / let them — for first and third person suggestions or soft commands). The negation system has two branches: δεν negates indicative verbs ('Δεν πάω' = I don't go), while μην negates subjunctive and imperative forms ('Μην πας' = Don't go). Additional particles of high frequency include ούτε (not even; neither — often paired: ούτε...ούτε), μόνο (only), και (and), αλλά (but), ή (or), όμως (however — usually placed after the first word of the clause), γιατί (because / why), άρα (therefore / so — marks a logical conclusion), and δηλαδή (that is / namely / in other words — introduces clarification). Unlike conjunctions, particles like θα and να cannot be separated from the verb they govern by anything other than a clitic pronoun.
sections.tip: Θα and να are the two most critical particles at B1 level. Θα + present stem forms the future: θα πάω (I will go). Να + present stem forms the subjunctive, used after verbs of wanting, needing, and hoping: θέλω να πάω (I want to go). Ας + verb makes a soft imperative for 1st or 3rd person: ας πάμε (let's go), ας φύγει (let him leave).

examples.en: I will go to the market tomorrow.
Don't be late — we will wait for you.
I don't know even one word of German.
He was tired, therefore he left early.

exercises.prompt: Which particle do you place before a verb to mark the future tense in Greek?
exercises.explanation: Θα is the future/conditional marker in Greek. It is placed directly before the verb: θα πάω (I will go), θα φάμε (we will eat). Να marks the subjunctive mood, ας forms soft imperatives, and μην is a negative particle.

exercises.prompt: Which particle introduces a clarification or paraphrase, meaning 'that is' or 'in other words'?
exercises.explanation: Δηλαδή means 'that is / namely / in other words' and is used to rephrase or clarify: Έφυγε, δηλαδή δεν είναι εδώ. (He left, meaning he is not here.) Άρα expresses logical consequence, όμως means 'however', and μόνο means 'only'.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Let's go to the beach!' using the correct Greek particle?
exercises.explanation: Ας + first person plural verb forms a suggestion or soft imperative meaning 'let's'. Ας πάμε! = Let's go! Να πάμε would be a subjunctive clause (that we go), θα πάμε states a future fact (we will go), and μην πάμε means 'let's not go' (negative).

sections.title: Coordinating Conjunctions
sections.explanation: Coordinating conjunctions link two elements of equal grammatical rank: two nouns, two adjective phrases, two main clauses, etc. The core set in Greek: και (and — the most common conjunction, also used in the sense of 'even' in some contexts), αλλά (but — general contrast), μα (but — more colloquial/emphatic than αλλά), ή (or — for alternatives), ούτε...ούτε (neither...nor — requires δεν before the verb in the first clause), είτε...είτε (either...or — expresses a choice between alternatives without preference), ενώ (while / whereas — used to contrast two simultaneous or opposing situations), όμως (however — typically placed after the first word or phrase of the second clause, not at the beginning), παρόλο που (although / even though — concessive; introduces a clause that contrasts with what is expected), and ακόμα και (even — for emphasis, meaning 'even X'). Position matters: και and αλλά appear between the two elements; όμως is postpositive (after the first word); ενώ begins a new clause. Παρόλο που, though semantically concessive, is treated here as a coordinator between two contrasted ideas at B1 level.
sections.tip: A common error is placing όμως at the very start of a sentence. Instead, put it after the first stressed word: Εγώ όμως δεν συμφωνώ. (I, however, don't agree.) Also note that ούτε...ούτε requires δεν before the main verb: Δεν τρώω ούτε κρέας ούτε ψάρι. (I eat neither meat nor fish.)

examples.el: Μου αρέσει η μουσική και ο χορός.
Ήθελα να έρθω, αλλά ήμουν κουρασμένος.
examples.en: I wanted to come, but I was tired.
Do you want tea or coffee?

examples.el: Είτε έρθεις είτε όχι, θα διασκεδάσουμε.
examples.en: Whether you come or not, we will have fun.

examples.el: Εγώ όμως πιστεύω ότι έχεις δίκιο.
examples.en: I, however, believe that you are right.

examples.el: Ακόμα και ο δάσκαλος δεν ήξερε την απάντηση.
examples.en: Even the teacher didn't know the answer.

exercises.prompt: Which conjunction pair is used for 'neither...nor' in Greek?
exercises.explanation: Ούτε...ούτε means 'neither...nor'. It requires δεν before the main verb: Δεν μιλά ούτε ισπανικά ούτε γαλλικά. (She speaks neither Spanish nor French.) Είτε...είτε means 'either...or'.

exercises.prompt: Where in a sentence is the conjunction όμως typically placed?
options: Between the subject and verb only
Always at the very beginning of the sentence
At the end of the sentence
After the first word or phrase of the clause
exercises.explanation: Όμως is postpositive — it follows the first stressed element in the clause rather than opening it: Εγώ όμως δεν ήρθα. (I, however, didn't come.) Placing it at the very start is unnatural in standard Greek.

exercises.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses ενώ to express a contrast?
options: Εγώ μελετώ ενώ και αυτός παίζει.
Εγώ ενώ μελετώ αλλά αυτός παίζει.
exercises.explanation: Ενώ (while / whereas) introduces a contrasting clause. It is placed at the boundary between the two clauses: Εγώ μελετώ, ενώ αυτός παίζει. (I study, while he plays.) The other options have incorrect word order or redundant conjunctions.

sections.title: Subordinating Conjunctions
sections.explanation: Subordinating conjunctions introduce a dependent (subordinate) clause that cannot stand alone. Greek subordinating conjunctions: ότι and πως both mean 'that' and introduce indirect statements after verbs of saying, thinking, and knowing (Νομίζω ότι έχεις δίκιο. — I think that you are right. Both ότι and πως are interchangeable here, with πως being slightly more colloquial). Γιατί (because) introduces a causal clause. Αν (if) introduces a conditional clause. Όταν (when) introduces a temporal clause; note it takes indicative, unlike the English confusion with 'if'. Ενώ (while) introduces a temporal or concessive clause with ongoing action. Αφού (since / after — causal or temporal; context distinguishes) introduces either a reason or a sequence. Μόλις (as soon as) introduces a punctual temporal clause. Μέχρι να (until — note: να marks subjunctive, so the dependent verb is in subjunctive mood) introduces a temporal boundary. Για να (in order to — purpose clause; verb following is subjunctive) expresses purpose. Παρά (rather than / instead of — used in comparisons of preference, typically with subjunctive: παρά να...) expresses a preference against. Εφόσον (since / provided that — used for conditions that are assumed to be true or for causal explanation in more formal registers) introduces a conditional or causal clause.
sections.tip: The distinction between αν and όταν is important: αν = if (uncertain condition), όταν = when (certain future event or recurring situation). Αν έρθεις, θα σε δω. (If you come, I will see you — uncertain.) Όταν έρθεις, θα σε δω. (When you come, I will see you — expected.) Purpose clauses with για να always require the subjunctive: Σπουδάζω για να μάθω. (I study in order to learn.)

examples.en: I think that you are right.
He didn't come because he was sick.

examples.el: Αν έχεις χρόνο, ελά να τα πούμε.
examples.en: If you have time, come and let's talk.

examples.el: Μελετώ για να περάσω τις εξετάσεις.
examples.en: I study in order to pass the exams.
As soon as it finishes, I will let you know.

exercises.prompt: Which conjunction is used for purpose clauses meaning 'in order to'?
exercises.explanation: Για να introduces a purpose clause and is always followed by the subjunctive: Πηγαίνω στο γυμναστήριο για να γυμναστώ. (I go to the gym in order to exercise.) Μόλις = as soon as, αφού = since/after, εφόσον = since/provided that.

exercises.prompt: What is the difference between αν and όταν in Greek?
options: Αν means 'if' (uncertain condition); όταν means 'when' (certain or recurring event)
Αν is only used in past tense; όταν only in future tense
Αν means 'when' (certain event); όταν means 'if' (uncertain condition)
exercises.explanation: Αν introduces a conditional clause where the outcome is uncertain: Αν έρθεις, θα χαρώ. (If you come, I will be happy.) Όταν introduces a temporal clause where the event is expected: Όταν έρθεις, θα φάμε. (When you come, we will eat.) They are not interchangeable.

exercises.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses μέχρι να to mean 'I will wait until he arrives'?
exercises.explanation: Μέχρι να requires the subjunctive form of the verb. Θα περιμένω μέχρι να έρθει. (I will wait until he comes.) The verb έρθει is the subjunctive/aorist stem form. Using ήρθε (past indicative) or combining with ότι is incorrect.
